sources_note: Growing guidance on this page draws on Australian and international horticultural research, state agriculture departments, and recognised jujube growers and grower associations. Chinese jujube (Ziziphus jujuba) is the deciduous, temperate species grown here, not the evergreen tropical Indian jujube (Ziziphus mauritiana).

core.intro: Jujube (Ziziphus jujuba), also called Chinese date or red date, is one of the toughest fruit trees you can plant. It is deciduous, intensely drought and heat hardy, and shrugs off poor, sandy, clay, alkaline and even salty soils that defeat most fruit trees. The catch is climate: jujube wants a hot, dry summer to ripen well and only a little winter chill, so it rewards inland and Mediterranean districts and disappoints on the humid coast. The state sections below cover the climate, regions, harvest timing and pests where you are; the notes here apply anywhere in the country.

sections.heading: Choosing a variety
sections.body: Jujube cultivars split into fresh-eating types, eaten crisp like a sweet little apple, and drying types, left to wrinkle into a chewy date. For fresh eating the standouts are Honey Jar (small, intensely sweet and juicy, very early), Li (large, round, crunchy, the classic first tree), Sugar Cane (sweet and crunchy), Shanxi Li and Redlands (large and sweet), and Silverhill (very late, sweet). The best drying varieties are Lang (large, pear-shaped, processes well), Ta Jan and Admiral Wilkes. Several earn their place both ways: GA866 (large, very high sugar), Si Hong, Chico, Tiger Tooth and the thornless Sherwood. Spread your harvest by mixing an early type (Honey Jar, Chico) with a mid and a late one. Note that some cultivars carry hooked or straight thorns while others, such as Sherwood, are nearly thornless.

sections.heading: Pollination: do you need two trees?
sections.body: Most jujubes will set some fruit on their own, and several widely grown cultivars (Li and Shanxi Li among them) are reliably self-fertile, so a single tree can crop. The important nuance is that cross-pollination from a second cultivar lifts fruit set and yield, and a few cultivars set poorly without a pollen partner and an active insect (jujube flowers are small and worked by ants, bees and other insects, not the wind). The practical rule from the research is to plant two or more different cultivars where you have room, which is easy here because nurseries sell a wide range. If you only have space for one, choose a dependable self-fertile variety such as Li.

sections.heading: Planting and soil
sections.body: Jujube needs full sun and good drainage, and beyond that it is remarkably unfussy: it crops on sand, loam or clay across a wide pH range of about 5.0 to 8.5, tolerates alkaline and saline ground, and survives in poor soils that starve other trees. It will not thank you for waterlogging, so avoid any spot where water pools and plant on a low mound if your soil is heavy. The tree is deciduous and leafs out very late in spring, which lets it dodge late frosts, and dormant wood is extremely cold hardy (Chinese northern types are reported to take around minus 30 degrees Celsius), so cold is rarely the limiting factor. A grafted, container-grown tree can go in through most of the year if you keep the water up over its first summer (see our guide on when to plant).

sections.heading: Water and feeding
sections.body: Jujube is one of the most drought-hardy fruit trees grown here, with deep, wide roots, and an established tree survives on very little water (one Californian grower applied less than half the water he gave his citrus). For good fruit, though, it does need some irrigation: water young trees through their first few summers and water bearing trees while the fruit is filling, because a tree run too dry sets and holds less fruit. Drainage still matters more than volume, since jujube hates wet feet. Feeding is modest: jujube is a light feeder, and the research on its fertiliser needs is genuinely limited, so do not over-think it. Hold off feeding a newly planted tree until it leafs out, then give an established tree a complete fertiliser plus compost or well-rotted manure in spring. Go easy on nitrogen, which drives leafy growth at the expense of fruit. No reliable jujube-specific NPK rate has been published, so be wary of any precise number you see quoted online.

sections.heading: Thorns, suckers and rootstock
sections.body: Named jujube cultivars are grafted, usually onto a seedling of the wild sour jujube (Ziziphus jujuba var. spinosa, also sold as Z. spinosa), which is hardy and easy to raise but does not come true from seed. The thing to know before planting is that jujube suckers freely from its roots, sometimes popping up metres from the trunk. Those suckers grow from the rootstock, so they are the wild sour jujube, not your named variety: they are far thornier, carry smaller leaves, and will never bear the fruit of the grafted tree. Mow or dig them out while they are young, avoid disturbing the soil over the roots, and consider a large pot if you want to sidestep suckering altogether. Growers in the business actually prize the suckers, digging them up each winter to use as rootstock for next season's grafts.

sections.heading: Harvest, fresh and dried
sections.body: Jujube is precocious: a grafted tree often sets a few fruit in its first year or two and a worthwhile crop by about the fourth or fifth year. The fruit ripens green, then turns yellow-green with mahogany spots that slowly spread, and finally a deep red-brown all over. For fresh eating, pick at the crisp stage between yellow-green and full red, when the flesh is sweet and apple-like; for drying, leave the fruit to colour fully and wrinkle on the tree, when it becomes a chewy, date-like red date with sugar climbing as high as 70 to 75 percent. Many varieties can be picked slightly under-ripe and will sweeten on the bench. Dried jujubes (the Asian-grocery red dates) store and travel well and are used in cooking, tea and traditional remedies.

sections.heading: Buying tips
sections.body: Buy a named, grafted tree rather than an unnamed seedling: jujubes do not come true from seed, so a seedling gives unpredictable fruit and may just be rootstock-quality sour jujube. Check the label for the cultivar name, whether it is a fresh or drying type, and whether it is thorny or thornless. Because nearly every named jujube fruits acceptably alone but crops better with a partner, it is worth buying two different cultivars at once. The in-stock table above shows the named varieties available right now and what they cost.

faqs.q: How soon will a jujube tree start bearing fruit?
faqs.a: Quickly, for a tree. A grafted jujube is precocious and often sets a handful of fruit in its first year or two, building to a solid crop by about the fourth or fifth year. A seedling takes longer and the fruit is a gamble, which is why a named, grafted plant is worth paying for.

faqs.q: Can I grow a jujube in a large pot?
faqs.a: Yes, and a pot has a bonus: it pens in the roots and stops the tree sending up suckers across the garden. Use a free-draining mix in a big container, give it the sunniest, hottest spot you have, water through summer because pots dry out fast, and feed lightly in spring. Repot or refresh the top of the mix every couple of years.

faqs.q: How big does a jujube tree get, and can I keep it small?
faqs.a: Left alone a jujube can reach about 8 to 10 metres, but it takes pruning well and is easily held to 3 or 4 metres for a backyard, which also makes netting and picking simpler. It is an attractive tree with glossy leaves that turn butter-yellow before dropping in autumn, so many people grow it as much for its looks as its fruit.

faqs.q: My jujube dropped its leaves and lots of little twigs in winter. Is it dying?
faqs.a: No, that is normal. Jujube is deciduous, so it loses its leaves every autumn and leafs out again late in spring. It also sheds many of its short fruiting twigs each winter, which can look alarming but is just how the tree works. You have not over-watered or under-fed it; new fruiting wood appears in spring.

WA.intro: Growing jujubes in Western Australia. WA is one of Australia's two leading jujube states, and the fit is excellent: the hot dry summers and mild winters of the south-west and the warm inland are close to the tree's ideal, and jujube laughs off the heat, drought and poor soils that trouble other fruit trees here.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Jujube is made for WA's drier country. It wants a hot, dry summer to ripen its fruit, needs only a low winter chill to break dormancy, and tolerates extreme heat, drought, and alkaline or salty soils, all of which describes much of agricultural WA. The south-west's Mediterranean pattern of hot dry summers and mild wet winters suits it, and the warm semi-arid interior suits it even better, provided the tree gets some irrigation while the fruit is filling. The one climate jujube dislikes is humid and wet at harvest, which is rarely WA's problem.

sections.heading: Where jujubes grow in WA
sections.body: DPIRD records jujube being grown in the Perth Hills, the northern Rangelands, the South West and the Great Southern, and there are established commercial orchards in the South West and the Great Southern. The warm, dry Wheatbelt, the Mid West around Geraldton and the Gascoyne all suit the tree's love of heat and tolerance of dry, alkaline ground. WANATCA records WA growers importing and trialling dozens of Chinese and Russian cultivars through quarantine over the years, so the local pool of proven varieties is unusually deep.

sections.heading: Harvest window in WA
sections.body: WA's summer heat brings the crop on early: picking generally starts from around the beginning of March and runs over several weeks, with early cultivars like Honey Jar and Chico first and very late ones such as Silverhill and Admiral Wilkes finishing the season. Pick fresh-eating fruit at the crisp red-brown stage, and leave drying cultivars on the tree to wrinkle.

sections.heading: Pests in WA
sections.body: The main pest is Mediterranean fruit fly, which is established across most of WA's growing districts and does use jujube as a host: DPIRD lists registered Medfly baits and cover sprays specifically for jujube, so net the tree or run a baiting and trapping program as the fruit ripens. WA has no established Queensland fruit fly and eradicates any incursion, so Medfly is the one to plan around. Birds, especially parrots, are the other regular raider, which is why WA orchards often net. The old claim that jujube has no serious pests in Australia does not hold up here.

sections.heading: Buying and shipping to WA
sections.body: WA regulates live plants brought in from other states: jujube must be permitted on the WA Organism List and meet entry conditions, which usually means treated, certified, and washed free of all soil, which is why many eastern nurseries will not post live trees here. Being deciduous and sold bare-rooted and dormant, jujube is easier to move than a potted evergreen, but it is not exempt from quarantine. The simplest path is locally grown stock, and because WA is a major jujube state that is widely available. The in-stock table above is filtered to sellers that can ship to WA.

faqs.q: Which jujube varieties are a safe bet in Western Australia?
faqs.a: Almost any will grow in WA's warmth, so choose by use. For fresh eating, Honey Jar, Li, Sugar Cane and Shanxi Li are dependable; for drying, Lang and Ta Jan; and the thornless Sherwood is worth seeking out if you would rather not deal with spines. Plant two different cultivars for a heavier crop and a longer picking season.

faqs.q: Do jujubes need much watering through a WA summer?
faqs.a: Less than you would think. An established jujube is deeply drought-hardy and gets by on very little, but it sets and holds far more fruit if you give it a deep drink every week or two while the fruit is filling through the dry summer. Mulch well, water young trees through their first few summers, and ease off once the fruit is picked.

QLD.intro: Growing jujubes in Queensland. Jujube is a hot, dry climate tree, so in Queensland it belongs in the warm, dry inland rather than the humid coast or the wet tropics, where damp weather at harvest spoils the fruit and lifts disease pressure.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Jujube needs a hot summer to ripen and only a little winter chill, both of which the Queensland inland supplies, but it does badly in humidity: in damp coastal and tropical air the ripening fruit is prone to splitting and to yeast and mould, and quality drops. The practical line is that the drier and sunnier your site, the better jujube performs, so it is a tree for the inland and the elevated, low-humidity districts rather than the wet coast. On the coast, treat it as a backyard experiment, choose firm fresh-eating cultivars and pick them promptly.

sections.heading: Where jujubes grow in QLD
sections.body: The dependable jujube country in Queensland is the warm, dry inland: there is a commercial jujube operation in the Western Downs near Goondiwindi, on the southern inland border. The Granite Belt around Stanthorpe, Queensland's highest and coolest fruit district, has the winter chill and low summer humidity jujube likes, though it is grown there far less than apples and stone fruit. Production thins out quickly towards the humid coast and disappears in the wet tropics.

sections.heading: Harvest window in QLD
sections.body: Queensland's warm inland brings jujube on early, with the main picking through about February and March, ahead of the cooler southern states. Early cultivars such as Honey Jar and Chico open the season and later types follow. In a humid year, pick fresh-eating fruit as soon as it colours rather than leaving it to wrinkle on the tree.

sections.heading: Pests in QLD
sections.body: Queensland fruit fly is endemic right through eastern Australia and is most active in humid conditions, so it is the insect Queensland growers ask about first. Interestingly, Chinese jujube is not on Business Queensland's published list of Queensland fruit fly host fruits, and the national biosecurity assessment of imported jujube lists other flies as its pests, so it appears to be at most a minor host. Even so, at least one eastern grower reports fruit fly attacking jujube, so in a Qfly district it is prudent to net or bait rather than assume immunity. Birds are the other regular raider.

faqs.q: Is it worth trying a jujube in Brisbane or on the Queensland coast?
faqs.a: You can grow the tree, but reliable, high-quality fruit is harder near the humid coast, where damp weather as the fruit ripens encourages splitting and mould. If you want to try, give it the hottest, most open, best-drained spot you have, choose a firm fresh-eating cultivar, and pick the fruit as soon as it colours instead of leaving it to dry on the tree.

faqs.q: Do jujubes drop their leaves in Queensland's warm climate?
faqs.a: Yes. Jujube is deciduous everywhere, even in the warm subtropics, so a Queensland tree still drops its leaves in autumn and stands bare through winter before leafing out late in spring. That is normal and not a sign of stress, and the late leafing helps it avoid any frost in the inland districts.

NSW.intro: Growing jujubes in New South Wales. Jujube thrives on long, hot, dry summers and tolerates drought, frost and poor soils, so across NSW it is a tree for the warm inland rather than the humid coast, where a dry ripening autumn gives the best fruit.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Jujube suits the warm, dry inland of NSW: it wants a hot summer to ripen, needs only a little winter chill, and copes easily with the drought, heat and frost of the interior. The humid coast and ranges are more marginal, because damp weather as the fruit ripens encourages splitting and mould and dulls the flavour, so the further inland and the drier the site, the better the result. The tree's deep roots and extreme drought tolerance make it a sensible low-water choice for inland gardens and small farms.

sections.heading: Where jujubes grow in NSW
sections.body: Jujube is less established commercially in NSW than in the leading states of WA and South Australia, where most of the small but growing Australian industry sits, but its needs point clearly to the warm inland: the Central West around Mudgee and the Riverina have the hot dry summers, winter chill and free-draining soils the tree wants. The humid coast is the wrong end of the state for it, though a sheltered, sunny, well-drained backyard spot can still produce fruit for a home grower. Nurseries on the coast supply the trees even where the best growing country is inland.

sections.heading: Harvest window in NSW
sections.body: In NSW jujube ripens through autumn, broadly February to April, earlier in the hot inland and later in cooler districts. Early cultivars such as Honey Jar and Chico start the season and late types such as Silverhill finish it. Pick fresh-eating fruit at the crisp red-brown stage and leave drying cultivars to wrinkle, weather permitting.

sections.heading: Pests in NSW
sections.body: Queensland fruit fly is endemic across coastal and inland NSW and is the pest growers watch for, and it is busiest in humid conditions. Chinese jujube does not appear on the official Queensland fruit fly host list and seems to be at most a minor host, but it is not immune, so in a Qfly area net the tree or run traps and bait as the fruit ripens rather than risk a stung crop. Birds, especially parrots, will strip ripe fruit and are often the bigger problem, so netting earns its keep.

faqs.q: Will a jujube fruit well on the NSW coast?
faqs.a: It will grow and may fruit, but the humid coast is not where jujube shines: damp weather as the fruit ripens causes splitting and mould and softens the flavour. You will get better fruit inland in the warm, dry country. On the coast, pick the hottest, most open, best-drained position you have and choose a fresh-eating cultivar you can pick firm.

faqs.q: Are jujubes a good low-care tree for a dry inland NSW property?
faqs.a: They are one of the best. Once established, jujube is exceptionally drought-hardy, tolerates poor and salty soils, handles frost when dormant, and has few serious pests beyond fruit fly and birds, so it suits an inland block where water is scarce. Just keep some water up while the fruit is filling and stay on top of root suckers.

VIC.intro: Growing jujubes in Victoria. The hot, dry northern inland of Victoria suits jujube well, while the cooler, wetter south is more marginal because the fruit ripens late and damp autumn weather can spoil it.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Victoria's hot, dry north suits jujube: it gives the summer heat the tree needs to ripen its fruit, the winters easily supply the small amount of chill it wants (growers report a requirement of only a few hundred hours below 7 degrees), and the tree is fully frost hardy when dormant, leafing out too late in spring to be caught by frost. The cooler, wetter, more humid south is harder going, because the season is shorter and damp autumn weather as the fruit ripens can cause splitting and mould. So jujube is a tree for the warm Victorian inland far more than the cool maritime south.

sections.heading: Where jujubes grow in VIC
sections.body: Victoria's jujube heartland is the Sunraysia district in the far north-west, around Mildura and Irymple, where commercial growers crop the fruit under the region's brilliant, dry summer heat beside the Murray. The hot, dry Mallee and Wimmera and the northern districts towards the Goulburn Valley have the same broad climate. The cooler, wetter south and the high country are far less suited and are better left to apples, pears and stone fruit.

sections.heading: Harvest window in VIC
sections.body: Victoria picks a little later than the warm northern states because the cooler season slows ripening, with Sunraysia fruit sold fresh mainly from February to April and cooler districts running into autumn. Early cultivars such as Honey Jar and Chico lead, with later types following. Pick fresh-eating fruit crisp and leave drying types to wrinkle on the tree where the autumn stays dry.

sections.heading: Pests in VIC
sections.body: Queensland fruit fly has spread into northern Victoria and is the fruit fly to plan around, and it is most active in humid conditions. Chinese jujube is not on the official Queensland fruit fly host list and looks to be at most a minor host, but it is not immune, so in a declared Qfly area net or bait the tree as the fruit ripens. As elsewhere, birds are a reliable nuisance on ripe fruit, so netting does double duty.

faqs.q: Are jujube trees frost hardy in Victoria?
faqs.a: Very. A dormant jujube is one of the most cold-hardy fruit trees you can grow, taking hard frosts in its stride, and because it leafs out so late in spring it usually escapes frost damage to new growth. Frost is rarely the problem in Victoria; getting enough summer heat to ripen the fruit in the cooler south is the real limit.

faqs.q: Which jujubes suit Victoria's shorter, cooler season?
faqs.a: Lean on the earlier-ripening cultivars so the fruit colours before autumn cools off: Honey Jar and Chico are early, and Li is reliable. In the warm Sunraysia north you can ripen almost anything, including the late drying types; in cooler southern gardens, stick to early fresh-eating varieties and the hottest, most sheltered spot you have.
